titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 7 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfred Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/48

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7 Nov My own pr. I had intended to write quite a decent letter today , to smooth
some ruffled fur, but Russel is in bed with a bad cough,& I have to
take his place with the General who is visiting the Corps Com . I rather look forward to Miss L Dean's reply, & wonder if the PR
has developed very stilted legs too, or whether she has learnt not to stir up growly bw.
Russel has been seedy for some days & I have been trying to persuade him to go
to bed, but the Genl's order today only had the desired effect. He looks so pale
as a rule that I imagine he will soon get over it. A perfectly briliant day today after frosty weather last night &
if only we could carry on like this with not more that 3 days wet, we should
get through the winter well, but we must I suppose be moved somewhere
in the next four weeks, but as there seems to be some chance of a
German attack, we may possibly be kept instead of going to Serbia which
I had looked upon as a certainty tho' not others. I can talk fairly
freely until we get any hint & then it will be mum. It is very curious that Ed has never referred to a letter I sent
him about rumours & it is possible he hs not got it, but on
what grounds ist should be suppressed I cannot imagine. You are rather naughty about your plans, not one of your letters
gives a hint as to your stay at Rugby whether for the night or
weekend or what , nor about dates for 24 HPG tho miss says you go there next
Friday. I did answer about the mattress , which is A 1 . It only has 2
compartments to Col Russell's 3 but as my weight is less & more distributed
this should not matter.
I argue about the splitting up of
the Divnbut this is being done to others out here & I really hope
only temporary. B Smith declares he is convinced that the RC influence
is being put in here on purpose tho I am sure that neither Carson nor
Maguire have anything to do with it. It certainly is curious that of the last
5 appointments 4 should have ben RCs! Many thanks for the Rubber
report which I return. I should like an account of the meeting.
Rubber is the one thing that is bouncing now & if only one didn't
foresee that it will puncture after the heavy war demands cease, I we
could face the future with equanimity. As it is we must use the
present fat years like Joseph & the pr is most good in realizing this. What a compliment I paid you in my remarks about Henry, but I
gather that your reply is that grey hairs have only begun to grow since you
were married ! Joy says that because she knows it is impossible to keep you in
your place, but I have better hopes of Alicewho knows from experience how
to deal firmly with children What a nice letter of Mrs Richardson's ! I have heard from Craig
a very nice letter which I will forward later. He hints that the Govt
is going to compromise with Germany. If so I am not sure that I would
not wish to be no longer an Englishman. & have
both written sadly: their own positions are most unsatisfactory, & the
in which they are finding reserves for the 16th&not for
us is perfectly ---able
I must be off now. The is still the entire stars of the p r espescially
at nights. More so than ever Your Ownbw <signed<Wilfrid B Spender
